currentUse: Privately owned former church, kept largely intact as a church interior by owner Robert Damyanovich, who lives in the adjacent former rectory; not in religious or commercial use as far as sources show
summary: St. John the Baptist (Šv. Jono) Lithuanian parish in Coaldale closed on July 15, 2008, when it was merged with five other Panther Valley parishes into the new Saint Katharine Drexel Parish in Lansford, per the successor parish's own history page. After the closure the diocese sold the church to a local man, Robert Damyanovich, who had married his Lithuanian wife there and baptized his children there; he lives in the rectory and has left the church mostly as it was, according to Global True Lithuania's coal-region heritage survey. The same source reports that after the sale the diocese tried to remove the stained-glass windows and the 1914 cornerstone, but the new owner and former parishioners fought this off — though the diocese did strip the Lithuanian donors' names from the windows. Damyanovich was interviewed in June 2023 for the Destination Lithuanian America project (TrueLithuania YouTube channel), which presents him as having saved the church by purchasing it. The canonical successor chain continued: St. Katharine Drexel Parish itself closed in June 2016 and its people were absorbed into St. Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley in Summit Hill, which an August 2025 Times News article describes as the last standing Catholic church in the four Panther Valley towns. The parish history also records that the original 1914 wooden church burned during Easter Mass on April 1, 1920, and the present building was dedicated July 4, 1921.

developments.headline: Parish closed in Allentown diocese consolidation
developments.detail: St. John the Baptist Parish, established 1914 for Coaldale's Lithuanian Catholics, closed on July 15, 2008, merging with five other area parishes into the newly formed Saint Katharine Drexel Parish in Lansford, per the successor parish's history page.

sources.title: Saint Katharine Drexel — parish history
sources.publisher: Saint Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley (stjscatholicchurch.org)

developments.headline: Diocese sold the church to local resident Robert Damyanovich, who preserved it
developments.detail: After the closure the church was bought by a local man attached to it because he married his Lithuanian wife and baptized his children there. He lives in the rectory and left the church mostly as it was. After the sale the diocese attempted to remove the stained-glass windows and the cornerstone; the owner and former parishioners resisted, though the diocese removed the Lithuanian donors' names from the windows.

sources.title: Shenandoah and southern Coal Region: Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Successor parish St. Katharine Drexel (Lansford) closed
developments.detail: Saint Katharine Drexel Parish occupied the former Saint Michael the Archangel Church building in Lansford until closing in June 2016; parishioners were absorbed into Saint Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley in Summit Hill — the current canonical home of the former St. John the Baptist community.

sources.title: Saint Katharine Drexel — parish history
sources.publisher: Saint Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley (stjscatholicchurch.org)

developments.headline: Destination Lithuanian America published video interview with the church's owner
developments.detail: The TrueLithuania YouTube channel (Destination Lithuanian America project) published "He bought a Lithuanian-American church in Coaldale, Pennsylvania | Robert Damyanovich," presenting Damyanovich — not Lithuanian himself — as having saved the Lithuanian church by purchasing it. This is the most recent direct evidence found of the building's preserved state.

sources.title: He bought a Lithuanian-American church in Coaldale, Pennsylvania | Robert Damyanovich
Interviews with diaspora Lithuanians
sources.date: undated (accessed 2026-07-16, page itself unreachable due to certificate error; details via search index and YouTube oEmbed)

developments.headline: St. John's legacy honored at Panther Valley Catholic 175th anniversary
developments.detail: At St. Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley's 175-years celebration, Msgr. Orsulak recalled the Lithuanians' strong presence in Schuylkill County and their founding of St. John the Baptist (1914). The article describes St. Joseph in Summit Hill (pastor Rev. Eric J. Gruber) as the last standing Catholic church in the four Panther Valley towns.

sources.publisher: Saint Joseph Parish of the Panther Valley (stjscatholicchurch.org)

He bought a Lithuanian-American church in Coaldale, Pennsylvania | Robert Damyanovich
Centennial celebration (SS. Peter and Paul, Tamaqua)

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Two refinements and two unverified archive claims. (1) Closure date: archive says "closed ~2009"; the successor parish's own history gives July 15, 2008, as the closure/merger date — the 2008 Allentown consolidation wave, not 2009. (2) Buyer name: archive has "R. Demyanovich"; TrueLithuania sources spell it Robert Damyanovich. (3) Purchase motive: archive says the sale was "to prevent warehouse use"; the opened sources instead give personal attachment as the motive (he married his Lithuanian wife and baptized his children there) — the warehouse-prevention framing was not found in any source opened and should be treated as unconfirmed. (4) The ~$24,000 sale price could not be verified in any opened source; property-record sites (Zillow, Redfin, PropertyReach, Long & Foster) all returned 403. Also a refinement to "Built 1914": the 1914 wooden church burned April 1, 1920; the present building was dedicated July 4, 1921 (successor parish history). Founding year 1914 and diocese_closed ending are confirmed.
gaps: Sale price (~$24,000 per archive) and exact sale year not found — real-estate/property-record pages were blocked (HTTP 403) and no press coverage of the transaction surfaced. Exact street address unverified: search snippets place the church at 229 W. Phillips St. (parishesonline.com) but the page itself returned no usable content; a separate listing for a brick church at 225 W. Phillips St. via churches4sale.net appeared in blocked real-estate indexes and could not be confirmed as (or ruled out as) this building. No 2024-2026 reporting on the building's condition or ownership was found; the most recent direct evidence of preservation is the June 2023 TrueLithuania interview, so current condition is inferred, not observed. The Destination Lithuanian America map entry (map.truelithuania.com) was unreachable due to a TLS certificate mismatch. Whether Damyanovich still owns the property today could not be verified. A 2011 Times News article says SS. Peter and Paul in Tamaqua (also Lithuanian) "now serves" Coaldale's closed parishes including St. John the Baptist — this pastoral-service claim sits alongside, not against, the canonical merger into St. Katharine Drexel/St. Joseph and was not further resolved.
